coredata.dc:title: Orange carbon dots for methylene blue detection in actual water samples, live cells and enoki mushroom
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yifan
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: The prompt and accurate detection of methylene blue (MB), a harmful cationic dye, is crucial for environmental monitoring. This research involved the creation of orange fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), specifically tailored for MB identification. Utilizing fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), the presence of MB significantly quenches the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Thus, CDs can detect MB with high selectivity, rapid response and enhanced sensitivity, achieving a low detection limit of 0.30 μM. Consequently, CDs proved to be effective tools for MB analysis in authentic aquatic environments and cellular contexts. We also developed a simple, portable detection system using a smartphone equipped with an RGB application, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.23 μM. Additionally, CDs have demonstrated effectiveness in detecting MB in enoki mushrooms. These findings highlight the future role of CDs as a glorious optical sensing platform for pollutant detection and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Methylene blue (MB)
Quantitative detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of MB in practical samples
Biocompatibility of CDs and intracellular detection of MB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs (inset: particle size range). (b) FT-IR spectrum. (c) Full XPS spectrum of CDs. HR XPS spectrum of (d) C1s. (e) N1s. (f) O1s. (g) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (inset: optical images of CDs solution under daylight and 365 nm UV light) (h) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (i) Emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelength.

(a) Selectivity of CDs toward MB (100 μM of 1. MB, 2. DA, 3. PHE, 4. MEL, 5. MgCl2, 6. NiCl2·6H2O, 7. FeCl2·4H2O, 8. KCl, 9. NaHSO3, 10. NaNO3, 11. Pb(NO3)2, 12. CDs, 13. NaAlO2, 14. Nd(NO3)3, 15. PRO, 16. Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 17. NaF). (b) FL spectra of CDs with MB added. (c) Photographic images of CDs under UV light with incremental MB (0–40 μM). (d) Linear correlation between the FL intensity change of CDs and the concentration of MB. (e) Interference assessment of various co-existing substances on the detection of MB by CDs, 3. DA, 4. PHE, 5. MEL, 6. MgCl2, 7. NiCl2·6H2O, 8. FeCl2·4H2O, 9. KCl, 10. NaHSO3, 11. NaNO3, 12. Pb(NO3)2, 13. NaAlO2, 14. Nd(NO3)3, 15. PRO, 16. Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 17. NaF. (f) Impact of treatment time with MB on the FL intensity of CDs.
High-content analysis (HCA) was performed on L929 cells incubated with (a) CDs (80 μg/mL), (b-e) MB at a concentration 10 to 100 μM. (f) the corresponding average FL intensity in (a-e).
(a) Photograph of enoki mushrooms taken by an in vivo optical imaging system. (b) Corresponding average FL intensity in (a). (c) Linear fitting curve between mean FL intensity and MB concentration.
(a) Preparation of CDs and applications of CDs for the determination of MB. (b) Detection of MB in tape water, lake water and enoki mushrooms.

head.ce:title: Orange carbon dots for methylene blue detection in actual water samples, live cells and enoki mushroom

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The prompt and accurate detection of methylene blue (MB), a harmful cationic dye, is crucial for environmental monitoring. This research involved the creation of orange fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), specifically tailored for MB identification. Utilizing fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), the presence of MB significantly quenches the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Thus, CDs can detect MB with high selectivity, rapid response and enhanced sensitivity, achieving a low detection limit of 0.30 μM. Consequently, CDs proved to be effective tools for MB analysis in authentic aquatic environments and cellular contexts. We also developed a simple, portable detection system using a smartphone equipped with an RGB application, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.23 μM. Additionally, CDs have demonstrated effectiveness in detecting MB in enoki mushrooms. These findings highlight the future role of CDs as a glorious optical sensing platform for pollutant detection and bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The widespread release of cationic dyes into aquatic systems is widely recognized to detrimentally impact the environment, leading to eutrophication and impeding the respiration of marine organisms [1–3]. With the expansion of the dyestuff industry, its effluent has emerged as a primary pollutant. Methylene blue (MB), a commonly used thiazine dye with considerable applications in agricultural and textile industries, is known for its toxicity. High concentration of MB is linked to cancer, congenital disabilities, and mutations in both humans and animals [4]. The World Health Organization has listed MB as a class III carcinogenic substance. Detecting MB in wastewater is crucial to enforce regulations and prevent unauthorized discharges by industrial entities. Therefore, various methods such as liquid chromatography [5], spectrometry [6–8], and surface-enhanced Raman scattering [9] have been devised for detecting and quantifying the levels of MB. Unfortunately, most of them are not inconvenient for microanalysis or practical application due to the shortcomings of high cost, low sensitivity, time consuming and complicated operation. Contrastingly, fluorometric technique has the merits of cost-effectiveness, high efficiency, selectivity and sensitivity, offering a straightforward and swift approach to identifying MB [7].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new category of nanocarbon materials, characterized as zero-dimensional entities with dimensions under 10 nm [10–12]. Due to their minimal toxicity, outstanding compatibility with biological systems, unique photoluminescent characteristics, and eco-friendliness [13–15], CDs have found broad applications across various domains, including catalysis [16,17], fluorescence imaging [18–22], optical devices [23,24], antisepsis [25–27], cancer therapy [28–30], and biosensing [31–34]. Statistically, the application of CDs is predominantly seen in sensing, where they are principally deployed in detecting environmental pollutants, such as metal ions [33,34], dyes [6,35], and antibiotics [36,37]. For instance, Xu et al. engineered fluorescent CDs to identify ClO− in water-based environments [38]. Gao et al. synthesized CDs that function as a selective fluorescent probe targeting Pt2+, Au3+, and Pd2+ ions [39]. Nevertheless, to our knowledge, there has been limited research on the use of CDs sensors for detecting MB.

In this study, we synthesized CDs utilizing benzene-1,2,4,5-tetraamine tetrahydrochloride (BTT) and eugenol as precursors through a straightforward solvothermal method (Scheme 1 a). Functioning effectively as a fluorescent sensor for MB, CDs demonstrated a strong linear correlation with the MB concentration between 5 and 30 μM, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.30 μM. We also pioneered a user-friendly technique utilizing a smartphone's RGB application for accurate identification of MB (Scheme 1a). Notably, CDs were able to detect MB in real water specimens, showcasing their practicality (Scheme 1b). Excessive MB can trigger oxidative stress in cellular systems, potentially compromising cellular viability and disrupting normal physiological functions. To address this concern, we employed CDs for real-time monitoring of intracellular MB dynamics (Scheme 1a). This approach gains particular significance considering that during enoki mushroom cultivation, environmental exposure or irrigation water contamination may lead to MB bioaccumulation in these edible fungi. Building on our cellular findings, we further adapted this CDs-based technology for sensitive MB detection in enoki mushroom matrices (Scheme 1b), establishing a versatile platform for both biological and agricultural applications. Collectively, these findings indicate that CDs hold potential for broad substance analysis applications.

Benzene-1,2,4,5-tetraamine tetrahydrochloride (BTT) and N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Tianjin Reagent Company. Eugenol, Methylene Blue (MB), Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), Phenylalanine (PHE), Melamine (MEL), Proline (PRO), KCl, NaHSO3, NaNO3, NaF, MgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Nd(NO3)3, NaAlO2, NiCl2·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetra-zolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). All the chemicals do not require further purification before use. Ultrapure water was prepared from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA).

CDs were produced using a solvothermal method. Briefly, a solution was prepared by dissolving equal amounts of BTT and eugenol, each at 100 mg, into 15 mL of DMF. Subsequently, the blend was placed into a Teflon-coated autoclave and subjected to heating at 180 °C for a duration of 8 h. The solution, after being allowed to reach room temperature, was then subjected to a 12 h dialysis process (MWCO = 500) to purify it from any remaining small, unreacted molecules. Ultimately, the sample underwent lyophilization, resulting in a dark brown solid.

Substance screening experiment: solutions of various substances (MB, DA, PHE, MEL, PRO, KCl, NaHSO3, NaNO3, NaF, MgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Nd(NO3)3, NaAlO2, NiCl2·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, at a concentration of 200 μM and volume of 1.5 mL) were introduced into a solution of CDs (20 μg/mL, 1.5 mL). The fluorescence spectra were measured at a 400 nm excitation. All experiments were conducted at room temperature.

Anti-interference experiment: solutions of various substances (MB, DA, PHE, MEL, PRO, KCl, NaHSO3, NaNO3, NaF, MgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Nd(NO3)3, NaAlO2, NiCl2·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, all at 300 μM, 1 mL each) were added to a 2 mL mixture of CDs (30 μg/mL) and MB (300 μM). The fluorescence spectra were measured at a 400 nm excitation. All experiments were conducted at room temperature.

Tap water was collected from the domestic supply at Changchun University of Technology, while lake water was sourced from South Lake, Changchun. All water samples underwent centrifugation and passed through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to eliminate impurities. Subsequently, a solution of MB at varying concentration (10–20 μM, 1.5 mL) was mixed into the CDs solution (20 μg/mL, 1.5 mL). The fluorescence spectra were measured at a 400 nm excitation. All experiments were conducted at room temperature.

Different concentration of MB was introduced into CDs solution, followed by capturing color photographs using a smartphone. The color RGB values were obtained using a smartphone application (Color picker APP).

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analysis revealed that CDs were well-dispersed in an aqueous solution, exhibiting monodisperse, spherical particles averaging 6.12 ± 1.22 nm in size (Fig. 1 a). The FT-IR spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 1b) shows a distinct peak at 3422 cm−1, which is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the –OH/N–H groups. The peak at 3213 cm−1, as well as those at 1628, 1497, 1403, 1267, and 1033 cm−1, correspond to the stretching vibrations of C–H, CO, CC, C–N, C–C, and C–O, respectively. Additionally, the absorption peak at 841 cm−1 is linked to the C–H bending vibration. The XPS full spectrum of CDs exhibits three prominent peaks corresponding to: carbon (C1s) at 284.0 eV, nitrogen (N1s) at 399.0 eV, and oxygen (O1s) at 532.0 eV. These peaks represent elemental compositions of 84.11 %, 1.06 %, and 14.82 %, respectively (Fig. 1c). The high-resolution (HR) XPS C1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) features peaks identified at 284.8 (C–C/CC), 286.3 (C–O/C–N), and 288.7 eV (CO). Additionally, N1s spectrum features two fitted bands (Fig. 1e) at 398.5 and 399.7 eV, attributed to C–N and amino nitrogen, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) showcases distinct peaks, decomposed at 531.9 (CO), 532.3 (C–O), and 533.5 eV (C–OH).

The luminescence characteristics of CDs were analyzed through photoluminescence (PL) and UV–Visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy. The absorption profile of CDs derived from UV–Vis spectrum exhibits peaks at 268 and 410 nm (Fig. 1g). These peaks are indicative of the π-π* transitions of C–C bonds and the n–π* transitions of C–O/CO bonds, respectively. The Fig. 1g illustration displays that CDs solution presents pale orange under daylight and emits bright orange fluorescence (FL) when subjected to 365 nm UV light. The FL spectra of CDs displayed their strongest prominent peak at 555 nm when they were excited by a 400 nm light source (Fig. 1h). Additionally, the emission peak remained at 555 nm over an excitation spectral span of 380 to 470 nm (Fig. 1i), indicating the typical excitation-independent emission behavior of CDs. The FL stability of CDs, an essential property, is demonstrated by their sustained FL intensity. Despite the CDs solution's extended exposure to UV light at 365 nm for 7 h, the FL intensity remained unchanged (Fig. S1a), suggesting the high photostability of CDs. Moreover, even in the presence of 600 μM NaCl, the FL intensity of CDs experienced minimal variation, indicating their stability to changes in ionic strength (Fig. S1b). The FL intensity of CDs was measured at various temperatures. Within the temperature range of 20–50 °C, the FL intensity of CDs remained relatively constant, showing no significant alterations (Fig. S1c). This finding indicates that CDs possess excellent temperature stability. When exposed to different pH conditions, the FL intensity of CDs exhibited variations (Fig. S2). However, in the near-neutral range, the change in FL intensity was not highly sensitive. Notably, the FL intensity reached its maximum at pH = 6.0. Consequently, in all subsequent experiments, solutions were prepared with ultrapure water, and the pH of these solutions was precisely adjusted to 6.0.

We investigated CDs as potential probes in detection applications. Fig. 2 a illustrates that 100 μM of MB can cause 95 % reduction of FL intensity from CDs. By comparison, none of the other substances induced a significant reduction of FL intensity from CDs. Therefore, CDs were deemed effective as selective probes for MB detection. As depicted in Fig. 2b, with a gradual increase in MB concentration, the FL intensity of CDs decreased progressively. The scatter plot of MB concentration versus FL intensity was shown in Fig. S3, where I0 signifies the innate emission intensity of CDs without MB, and I represent the intensity with MB present. Fig. 2c further reveals that increasing amounts of MB lead to a dampening of CDs' luminescence under a 365 nm UV light beam. Corresponding linear relationships were obtained at MB levels of 5–30 μM (Fig. 2d, I0/I-1 = 0.1224[MB] − 0.3769, R2 = 0.9985) and an LOD of 0.30 μM. Compared to other methods (Table S1), the CDs probe demonstrated greater sensitivity. Fig. 2e demonstrates the resilience of CDs against potential interference when sensing MB. The addition of other substances did not affect the detection of MB by CDs, verifying that CDs have strong selectivity and immunity to interference for MB detection. A swift quenching of CDs' FL, noted within a minute upon MB interaction, is showcased in Fig. 2f, thus underscoring the rapid detection capabilities of CDs with respect to MB.

As MB levels rise, the hue of CDs solution shifts from an orange to a rich blue when viewed in natural light (Fig. S4). Accordingly, we developed a strategy for the facile identification of MB using smartphone-based imaging. The smartphone determination platform is illustrated in Fig. S4. With the addition of MB (2.5–40 μM), CDs solution exhibited a continuous color change, which was promptly captured by the smartphone camera. Subsequently, the captured colors were converted into RGB values using a color recognition application. The MB concentration (2.5–25 μM) demonstrated a strong linear relationship with the R-value (Fig. S4, R = −5.3029[MB] + 143.3621, R2 = 0.9978) and an LOD of 0.23 μM. Quantitative visual measurement of MB via smartphone circumvents the need for bulky laboratory instruments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MB in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: To substantiate the reliability and accuracy of CDs in detecting real samples, we spiked tap water and lake water with various concentration of MB and conducted recovery experiments. Outcomes detailed in Table S2 reveal MB retrieval rates in tap water fell between 98.83 % and 101.45 %, accompanied by a relative standard deviation (RSD) beneath 0.6 %. Similarly, the recovery of MB in lake water was between 99.07 % and 102.46 %, with an RSD less than 0.55 %. These findings confirm that CDs are capable of detecting MB in actual water samples.

To unravel the nature of the interaction between CDs and MB, the quenching process was examined. FL quenching is categorized into static and dynamic types. The characteristics of static quenching are: (1) The FL lifetime of CDs unchanged pre- and post-quenching events. (2) Engagement with the quenching agent leads to modifications in the CDs' UV–Vis absorptive spectrum. In contrast, the hallmarks of dynamic quenching include: (1) The FL lifetime of CDs is altered subsequent to the quenching process. (2) The absorbance spectrum within the UV–Vis range for CDs remains consistent [11,40]. We examined the UV–Vis spectrometric profiles of CDs, MB, and CDs + MB mixture (Fig. S5a). The absorption spectrum of MB exhibited two peaks at 290 and 658 nm. For the CDs + MB mixture, three peaks appeared at 276, 410, and 660 nm, where the peaks at 276 and 410 nm originat from CDs. Thus, no new absorption band was observed in the CDs + MB mixture, suggesting that no new compounds was formed [41]. Consequently, the FL attenuation is not a result of static quenching. Furthermore, the FL lifetimes was 15.97 ns for CDs and 10.21 ns for the CDs + MB mixture (Fig. S5b). This change in FL lifetime upon MB addition suggests dynamic quenching by CDs [42].

Upon further detailed investigation, we observed that the UV–Vis absorption profile of MB exhibited partial overlap with the FL emission spectrum of the CDs (Fig. S5c). This overlap suggests that the mechanism of FL quenching might involve FRET [41]. Based on the FL lifetime data of CDs and the CDs + MB, we computed a FRET efficiency (E) of 36.07 %, according to Equation (1). And in this system, CDs serve as the energy donor (td), while the CDs + MB act as the donor–acceptor pair (td-a).
(1)
E = 1 -
t
d - a
/
t d

To further confirm that quenching of CDs by MB is due to FRET, we conducted theoretical calculations. In accordance with the principles laid out by Förster regarding non-emissive energy transfer, there are three essential prerequisites for FRET to occur [43]: 1. An effective overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor; 2. Proper orientation of the dipoles of the donor and acceptor; 3. A separation distance between the donor and acceptor ranging from 10 Å to 100 Å. The intermolecular distance was calculated using Equations (2–4), providing further support for our hypothesis.
(2)
E = 1 -
t
d - a
t d
= 1 /
1 +
r /
R 0
6
(3)
R 0
= 0.2108
κ
2
× Φ ×
n
R
- 4
× J
λ
1 / 6
(4)
J
λ
=
∫
0
∞
F D
λ
ε A
λ
λ
4
d λ

Thus, we calculated J(λ) to be 2.638 × 1017 M−1·cm−1·nm4, R0 to be 79.51 Å, and r to be 83.06 Å, all within the range of 10 to 100 Å. Accordingly, we hypothesize that an interaction exists between MB and CDs. MB likely serves as an energy acceptor and CDs as an energy donor, facilitating FRET that results in FL quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility of CDs and intracellular detection of MB
ce:section.ce:para: CDs exhibit impressive detection capabilities and show promise for practical applications. Consequently, we investigated their utility in biological contexts. Initially, MTT tests were performed to assess the biological compatibility of CDs. The cell viability remained above 80 % when treated with CDs across a concentration range of 0–100 μg/mL (Fig. S6). Furthermore, the hemolysis test was employed to investigate the blood compatibility of CDs. The results unequivocally demonstrate that no hemolysis occurs in the presence of CDs, even when the concentration of CDs reaches as high as 80 μg/mL. This finding firmly attests to the excellent blood compatibility of CDs (Fig. S7). This outcome substantiates the minimal cell toxicity of CDs and their significant potential for live cellular imaging applications.

Leveraging the outstanding FL properties of CDs, we extended our research to include live cell imaging experiments. Fig. 3 a shows that L929 cells displayed strong orange FL after a three-hour incubation with CDs at 80 µg/mL. When different concentration of MB from 10 to 100 µM were introduced, a noticeable reduction in the FL of the L929 cells was observed (Fig. 3b–e). Additionally, Fig. 3f presents a numerical evaluation of the changes in FL intensity depicted in Fig. 3b–e. These findings underscore the capability of CDs to serve as effective probes for the detection of MB within cells.

To further demonstrate that CDs can detect MB content in edible mushrooms, we immersed enoki mushrooms in a solution containing both CDs and MB. The CDs concentration remained constant at 100 µg/mL, whereas the levels of MB were adjusted in increments of 10 µM, starting at 10 µM and increasing to 50 µM. As presented in Fig. 4 a and b, with increasing concentration of MB, the FL intensity in the enoki mushrooms decreased progressively. Fig. 4c presents a quantitative plot of the FL intensity, which displayed a strong linear correlation with MB concentration ranging from 10 to 40 µM. These findings testify to the broad potential applications of CDs for MB sensing.

We synthesized CDs for MB detection using BTT and eugenol as precursors through a simple solvothermal method. CDs exhibit excellent water solubility and strong orange luminescence, making them effective fluorometric probes for selective MB sensing. Additionally, we developed an intuitive smartphone application that uses RGB analysis for rapid on-site MB quantification. The favorable biocompatibility and superior bioimaging capabilities of CDs also make them well-suited for intracellular MB detection. Moreover, CDs have demonstrated the ability to measure MB concentration in enoki mushrooms. The scalability and sensitivity of CDs offer promising potential for advancing from experimental research to practical applications, positioning them as strong candidates for optical nanoprobes in both sensing and bioimaging.
Yifan Sun: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Min Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Research Project of Science and Technology of the Education Department of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20250699KJ)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113531.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Molecules

Adv. Sci.
